body:
Seems more like a lang question to me than an opsem question -- from an opsem side, the relevant question is whether there is UB, and the answer is "no". But from a lang perspective, it may seem odd to have `unsafe` rules that depend on the place-vs-value distinction, as many programmers do not properly understand this distinction. But maybe it's good that they will wonder " :thinking: why is this safe?" and then can be taught about said distinction?

Also note that due to https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/117288, sometimes we do have UB just from constructing a place. That's a MIR building bug but we should probably fix it before accepting such code as safe. (Sorry I didn't remember this when we spoke earlier.)
author: RalfJung

body: 👍 yep, this could be blocked on #117288 then. I think we can probably T-lang-nominate this regardless for discussion.
author: compiler-errors

body: The code changes seem fine, so r=me on them if/when lang/opsem are happy with this.
author: nnethercote

body: Thinking about the `DEREF_NULLPTR` relaxation, I was pondering whether we should still lint -- but not say "this is UB" when it's not, of course -- on this as something that we might want you to do in a different way instead.  @compiler-errors convinced me that rustc is not the correct place for that, however, and a "clippy suspicious" or similar would be the place to do that instead, if useful.
author: scottmcm
traviscross

body:
So `let _ = *raw_ptr;` and `match *raw_ptr { _ => ... }` are not affected by this?

That does seem a bit inconsistent. It doesn't seem super useful to write code like that, but then it doesn't seem super useful to write `&raw const *ptr` either.
author: RalfJung

body: @RalfJung: No, that would need to have another special case in the THIR.

body:
> That does seem a bit inconsistent. It doesn't seem super useful to write code like that, but then it doesn't seem super useful to write &raw const *ptr either.

🤷 I'd still like to fix it, regardless, I think, especially now that #117288 is closed. I'd also like if `let _ = *raw_ptr;` weren't unsafe, but that's far more difficult for unsafeck to implement.

I mean, we intentionally changed it to require being in an `unsafe` block...

author: nnethercote

body: Procedurally it has been [approved](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/129248#issuecomment-2302506438) by t-lang. I'm not very familiar with THIR so it's hard for me to say whether the implementation matches the intent. We don't have a dedicated test for the cases that should be rejected, but tests/ui/lint/lint-deref-nullptr.rs and tests/ui/static/raw-ref-deref-without-unsafe.rs to cover the most important cases I can think of.

body:
looks a bit like a mix of 
clippy::useless_converison `let x: A = A.into()`
 clippy::unneccessary_cast `1_u32 as u32`
 clippy::redundant_clone /  unnecessary_to_owned  `let x: String= format!("a").to_string()`
author: matthiaskrgr

body: @matthiaskrgr I'm trying to gauge how common the root cause of the recent `time` breakage (a stray identity `.into()`) is in the ecosystem. [`clippy::useless_conversion`](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/blob/master/clippy_lints/src/useless_conversion.rs) is the right analogue, but that one [has the same issue that I partly side-stepped in this PR of being too eager and ignoring `cfg`s](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-clippy/issues/6466).
author: estebank

body:
@estebank Skimming over the results, everything being flagged looks like the lint is operating exactly as intended.

I personally think it'd be reasonable to make this warn-by-default.
author: joshtriplett

body: @joshtriplett I have some additional work I want to do to minimize false positives (once I get a `DefId` for either a type or a function, look at all its parents to see if any of them have `cfg` attributes, all the way to their root module) before this can be landed. Besides that, do you feel like those changes should be landed on clippy, or should we finish this lint and make the clippy one redundant?
author: estebank

body:
@estebank I would aim for directly getting this into rustc, rather than trying to go back and fix the clippy lint.

Do you think it'd be worth landing this as allow-by-default, to reduce the amount of work you need to do to keep this up to date as you work on the false-positive-reduction to make it warn-by-default?
author: joshtriplett

body:
> Do you think it'd be worth landing this as allow-by-default, to reduce the amount of work you need to do to keep this up to date as you work on the false-positive-reduction to make it warn-by-default?

Let me see what I can accomplish in the coming days. We might still want to make it allow-by-default at first, and go file PRs for the projects affected in the crater run, but then we can enable it after a (few?) cycle(s).

I just realized that we have no mechanism for "enable this lint as deny or warn on nightly but not on stable" as a way of getting feedback on lints from nightly users before it starts hitting stable users. The only way of doing that is making it warn-by-default and then patch beta to make it allow-by-default. It sounds like a reasonable thing to do for any "iffy" lints going forward.
author: estebank

body: @estebank Any thoughts about the possibility of landing this as allow-by-default? That would make it easier to start flagging this in various projects and patching them before starting to warn about it.
author: joshtriplett

body: @joshtriplett I've got a half baked attempt at making it reliable enough when encountering type aliases, but a version without that that is allow by default could be cooked up fairly quickly. Is that something that folks on the t-lang side would be ok with? I still would want to finish the more complex version in the near future so that it *can* be warn-by-default (we might want to have two versions, even because the latter version with the current approach I'm following would have false negatives, while the former would have false positives, neither can be perfect, unless we stopped eagerly transforming type aliases in the type system).
author: estebank

body:
@estebank I'd be really interested in an allow-by-default version even if it had some false positives; that'd be something to encourage crates to use.

To what degree is the "remember that this was once a type alias" version (not *leaving them untransformed* but just remembering that a type alias was involved) feasible? That's one bit worth of information: was this once a type alias or not? (Or, "remember that this was once a type alias tagged with some attribute", potentially.)
author: joshtriplett

body:
Just want to point out that `clippy::useless_conversion` does the simplistic version of this lint, that you can recommend crates use.

> To what degree is the "remember that this was once a type alias" version (not leaving them untransformed but just remembering that a type alias was involved) feasible?

We could add a new entry in `TypeFlags` (we're at 25 used out 32), but don't know t-types would be too happy about it. That's the "cheapest" change I can think of that could potentially work.
author: estebank

body:
> We could add a new entry in `TypeFlags` (we're at 25 used out 32), but don't know t-types would be too happy about it. That's the "cheapest" change I can think of that could potentially work.

I think it'd be worth considering, and smoke-testing with t-types to find out if it seems reasonable. I wouldn't expect it to be used to affect *resolution*, just linting like this.
author: joshtriplett

body:
@joshtriplett I believe I have a workable path forward now.

1) since I created the PR, some additional scaffolding was added to `#[cfg]` that let's me identify when an item was successfully selected, instead of completely eliding the attribute.
2) reused that same scaffolding to also account for `cfg_select!`, which allows me to have a single detection strategy that works for `c_int` and friends
3) through local testing it seems like the heuristic of ignoring all types that have type aliases that have been imported, and either the `use` or the `type` had a `cfg` works well enough (at the cost of false-negatives)
4) I'm going to perform a crater run, and some FIXMEs are left to address, but I'm otherwise happy with the results
5) we need to determine whether landing this in rustc and deprecate/make redundant `clippy::useless_conversion` is the best path forward (I believe it is)
6) Would you consider it a good idea to either add a second lint that is allow-by-default that *doesn't* have the filters, or allowing `clippy::useless_conversion` to pick those cases up, having more false positives but I believe no false negatives?
author: estebank

body:
This is a pattern that I am currently not addressing and am tempted in not addressing in the logic:

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/4804ad7e93e1b31f4605b7083871d0d3d85a2afe/library/std/src/sys/process/mod.rs#L1-L22

The modules for different platforms are behind cfg_select/cfg, re-exported with the same name, and they define different *types* (not just aliases). I'll need to figure out how to detect this without severely increasing the number of false-negatives. Hopefully crater will let us know how prevalent these are, but suspect there won't be that many. If I can come up with reasonable logic that would not trigger with this case, we could have a second more lenient lint that is error by default.
author: estebank

body:
This PR is rebased on top of https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/157218 for the `cfg_select!` changes. They are necessary for the lint to not trigger when using `c_int` and friends (because those it).

I am still waiting on the crater results, but I am satisfied with the current state of the patch. This is now ready for review.

As an update to https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/129249#issuecomment-4580811762, these two open questions for t-lang:

> - we need to determine whether landing this in rustc and deprecate/make redundant `clippy::useless_conversion` is the best path forward (I believe it is)
> - Would you consider it a good idea to either add a second lint that is allow-by-default that doesn't have the filters, or allowing `clippy::useless_conversion` to pick those cases up, having more false positives but I believe no false negatives?

The impact of this pattern is significant enough that I feel like it deserves to be warn-by-default in rustc itself.

I also split the lint into two, one for the expected case and another for this occurring withing a macro expansion. I was under the initial plan of detecting whether the macro was local and only warning if that was the case, but even in local macros there are cases where calling `.into()` only incidentally sometimes is unnecessary (even encountered cases in our own codebase).

More complex scenarios (like glob imports or two step imports where intermediary steps are conditionally compiled) are not handled, but I only encountered *1* such case. We'll see how much that happens in the wild, but expect it to be low enough to be able to move forward.
author: estebank

doc comments rustdoc::invalid-rust-codeblocks warn codeblock could not be parsed as valid Rust or is empty rustdoc::private-intra-doc-links warn linking from a public item to a private one rustdoc::redundant-explicit-links warn detects redundant explicit links in doc comments Lint groups loaded by this crate: name sub-lints ```

By reading the recent comments history (linking them here for convenience, [comment](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/129249#issuecomment-4593966545) and this [comment](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/129249#issuecomment-4754515434)), I'll try to roll someone from t-compiler.

author: apiraino

body:
>  I'll try to roll someone from t-compiler.

:+1: 

> LMK if this should first have a pass from T-lang

I *feel* like at this point we'd like *some* t-lang input to verify that the current set of checks is what we want, t-compiler (for impl) and t-libs-api (for the new lint surface), with the go-no-go laying mostly on the latter. I know that @joshtriplett will likely want to take another pass at this.

I *know* that we need to improve the wording of the lint. I also know that this code, as is, wouldn't have caught time's issue because that one was behind `|foo| foo.map(Into::into)`. I tried implementing a check for that, and noticed that closure bodies weren't being visited in the first place, so we'll need additional impl work for a comprehensive enough solution. *Edit*: addressed all of these points. The wording could still do with another iteration, but it has been improved slightly. The issue with the closure was fixed (by changing how the lint is registered) and `foo.map(Into::into)` now correctly triggers:

```
~/workspace/time$ cargo +dev build --features formatting
<snip>
warning: useless conversion to the same type: `format_item::Item<'_>`
  --> time/src/format_description/parse/mod.rs:84:20
   |
84 |         .map(|res| res.map(Into::into))
   |                    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
   |
   = note: this method call relies on the `impl<T> Into<T> for T` blanket implementation and type inference, which is a semver hazard as a new `impl Into<_>` that affects your type might be added in the future causing type inference errors
   = note: `#[warn(self_type_conversion)]` on by default
help: consider removing the conversion call
   |
84 -         .map(|res| res.map(Into::into))
84 +         .map(|res| res)
   |
<snip>
```

> there are so many comments from bots that I am not sure I am reading the whole history here

I love the bot-added link with all the comments :) https://triagebot.infra.rust-lang.org/gh-comments/rust-lang/rust/pull/129249

It'd make sense to make some progress on https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/157218 to make this PR easier to land too.

body:
The implementation looks pretty reasonable to me, but as this is an API decision:

r? libs-api

Is the intent here to replace the existing `copy_from_slice` and `clone_from_slice`? If so, those should be removed.
author: tgross35

body: I decided to not delete them since so many people are using these methods, but I can deprecate them depending on what people think is best.

body:
We discussed this in the libs-api meeting. We're happy with these changes, but we would also like to see similar changes for the `fill` (#117428) and `as_bytes` (#93092) methods so that all slice-related methods are turned into inherent methods. Note that `fill` will need to  be renamed since it conflicts with the generic `fill` method on slices (perhaps `fill_init`?).

Also, the old methods on `MaybeUninit` should be removed (in a separate PR) so that we don't have duplicate methods that do the same thing.
author: Amanieu

body:
Based on the above it seems like there are a few updates needed here.

author: rust-log-analyzer

body:
I updated the PR description to include the latest version of the changes I'm working on, and will mark for review when ready. But additionally going to point out that my main reasoning for *deprecating* rather than deleting the methods is mostly to avoid completely breaking nightly users, since I'd imagine these methods are very widely used. This way, nightly folks can at least get a message that tells them what to do, rather than just being gaslit into believing that the methods never existed.

That said, I'm also open to outright deleting them in this PR, since I'll have done the work of migrating the compiler and standard library code over anyway.

body:
@clarfonthey any chance you are willing to reconsider moving `fill` changes to another PR? I don't think there is any reason that change needs to happen at the same time.

Just asking because I'd like to start using this new API :)
author: tgross35

body: I mean, if you're cool merging this as-is without the fill methods, I'm fine splitting it. Just want to clarify which methods should be left in and which are left out.
author: clarfonthey

body: Specifically the changes `fill` -> `write_filled`, `fill_with` -> `write_with`, `fill_from` -> `write_iter`, since the names are the only things not okayed by someone from libs-api at https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/129259#issuecomment-2501783702. So everything except those three are 👍 from me.
